replication_study.hypothesis: Subjects in the two-stage (TS) multistage tournament exert higher effort in the first stage (effort_t1) than subjects in the one-stage (OS) tournament. Operational testable statement: mean(effort_t1 | treat==2) > mean(effort_t1 | treat==1).
replication_study.study_type: Experimental (laboratory) — observational analysis of experimental data

data_plan.source_type: Controlled laboratory experiment dataset (z-Tree session outputs converted to CSV and merged with questionnaire), provided as CSV
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Full available dataset in replication_data folder (no waves). Use all rows in FinalData.csv (N = 312). If replicating only the focal comparison, subset to treat==1 (OS) and treat==2 (TS).
data_plan.sample_size: 312 observations (rows) in FinalData.csv. (See data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv; dataset shape reported by the loader: 312 rows, 24 columns.)
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual subject (each row corresponds to a subject in a session)
data_plan.access_details: Dataset packaged with replication materials in data/original/28/input/replication_data. No external access restrictions for this copy. Original recruitment used ORSEE and z-Tree per post_registration.json, but those systems are not required to access these provided CSVs.

qualification.explanation: This dataset is qualified for replicating the focal claim because it contains the operational outcome (effort_t1) and the treatment indicator (treat) required to test the focal comparison TS first-stage vs OS. The dataset is produced from the same experimental protocol described in the post_registration.json (experimental implementation via z-Tree; between-subjects treatments OS and TS), and the provided DataDictionary.csv documents the variable definitions (e.g., treat coding and effort_t1 meaning). Files inspected: data/original/28/input/post_registration.json, data/original/28/input/replication_data/DataDictionary.csv, data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv.
qualification.similarity_to_original: High fidelity: post_registration.json documents the original experiment (z-Tree, OS and TS treatments, focal outcome = chosen effort (points)). The provided FinalData.csv includes 'effort_t1' (effort in stage 1) and 'treat' (1=OS, 2=TS) as defined in DataDictionary.csv (data/original/28/input/replication_data/DataDictionary.csv). The original claim and focal numerical comparisons (OS mean ~71.7; TS first-stage mean ~84.8; TS vs OS p=0.011) are recorded in initial_details.txt and post_registration.json (data/original/28/input/initial_details.txt and data/original/28/input/post_registration.json). These align directly with the variables available in FinalData.csv.
qualification.deviation_from_original: Deviations that make this a new sample or require caution: (1) Sample size differs: post_registration.json reports 320 subjects in the original study summary, while the provided FinalData.csv contains 312 rows (data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv). (2) Some personal-characteristic variables mentioned in the original (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive ability) are not present in FinalData.csv; instead only demographic/quiz fields (gender, major, year, age, open-ended 'decisions') appear (compare variables in post_registration.json with columns in FinalData.csv and DataDictionary.csv). (3) The original .do file comments state that identifying information from .sbj files was removed for ethics prior to release; the replication copy is already cleaned and anonymized (see data/original/28/input/replication_data/data cleaning and analysis.do comments). These deviations imply that some heterogeneity analyses from the original cannot be exactly reproduced but the focal test (TS vs OS on effort_t1) can be performed with high fidelity.

data_plan.notes: Key practical notes/caveats: (1) File DataDictionary.csv documents treat coding: treat = 1 (OS), treat = 2 (TS). (2) effort_t1 has no missing values in FinalData.csv (per dataset inspection). (3) Several questionnaire fields contain missing entries (gender, age, major have some NAs). (4) The provided Stata .do used absolute desktop paths; those were changed in the Python translation to use /app/data paths. (5) The original .do comments indicate that identifying info was removed prior to upload (see data cleaning and analysis.do in replication_data).

steps: 2) Verify variable types and codes (treat, effort_t1). Confirm treat coding matches DataDictionary (treat==1 => OS, treat==2 => TS).
3) Compute descriptive statistics (count, mean, median, SD, min, max) of effort_t1 overall and by treatment.

4) Perform the primary test: Mann-Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum / ranksum) comparing effort_t1 distributions for treat==2 (TS) vs treat==1 (OS), two-sided — this reproduces the original nonparametric test in the .do file and in the paper's reported claim.
5) For reference, run a Welch two-sample t-test on effort_t1 (two-sided) and compute mean difference (TS - OS) and Cohen's d.

6) (Optional) Run OLS regression: effort_t1 ~ I(treat==2) (+ controls age, gender dummies if present) to estimate treatment coefficient and standard errors (heteroskedasticity-robust). This approximates heterogeneity and adjusted comparisons, but is secondary to the preregistered nonparametric test.
7) Report results (means, medians, test statistics, p-values, effect sizes). Compare to original reported values (OS mean ~71.7; TS first-stage mean ~84.8; reported p=0.011).
8) Document any differences due to sample size, missing variables, or coding.
planned_method.models: Primary: Mann-Whitney U (two-sample rank-sum) comparing effort_t1 by treatment. Secondary: Welch two-sample t-test and OLS regression with robust SEs (effort_t1 as continuous outcome, treatment dummy as predictor).
planned_method.outcome_variable: effort_t1 (effort in stage 1, numeric points; column in FinalData.csv).
planned_method.independent_variables: Primary: treat (binary indicator; treat==2 is TS, treat==1 is OS). Secondary/controls: age, gender, year, major if available and desired.
planned_method.control_variables: Use controls sparingly. Potential controls available in FinalData.csv: age, gender, year, major (note: several of these have missing values). The original heterogeneity measures (competitiveness, risk attitudes, overconfidence, cognitive ability) are not present in this FinalData.csv, so those exact controls cannot be used (see deviation notes).
planned_method.tools_software: Python 3.10+, pandas, numpy, scipy, statsmodels (for regression), possibly matplotlib/seaborn for plots. The provided analysis script is in Python (data_cleaning_and_analysis__py.py) and uses pandas, numpy, scipy.

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Estimate mean(effort_t1 | treat==2) - mean(effort_t1 | treat==1) (difference in means); median differences and nonparametric rank differences; OLS treatment coefficient (effort_t1 on treatment dummy) as robustness.
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Primary statistical test: Mann-Whitney U test (two-sided) for ranksum. Secondary tests: Welch two-sample t-test for means (two-sided); t test on OLS coefficient with robust SEs.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion for analyses that include controls. For the primary test (effort_t1 by treat), there are no missing observations in effort_t1 or treat in FinalData.csv (per dataset inspection); if missing values appear, exclude rows with missing effort_t1 or missing treat. For regressions including demographics, drop observations missing those particular covariates but report N used.
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: The focal test is a single primary comparison (effort_t1 TS vs OS). No correction required for the primary hypothesis. If multiple secondary outcomes or multiple subgroup tests are performed, apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg as appropriate and report both raw and corrected p-values.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Primary inference: two-sided p < 0.05 indicates statistical significance for the Mann-Whitney U test. Directional expectation: TS > OS (original reported p=0.011 two-sided). Also report effect sizes and confidence intervals where applicable (e.g., mean difference and 95% CI from OLS or bootstrap).

files.data_cleaning_and_analysis__py.py: Python translation of the provided Stata .do preprocessing and focal analysis. Location: data/original/28/input/replication_data/data_cleaning_and_analysis__py.py. Reads /app/data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv, performs cleaning coercion to numeric types, computes descriptive statistics by treatment, runs Mann-Whitney U and Welch t-tests, computes mean difference and Cohen's d, and writes results to /app/data/replication_results.json and /app/data/replication_results.csv. This is the primary executable script for the replication.
files.FinalData.csv: Primary cleaned dataset for the replication analysis. Location: data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv. Contains variables including sessioncode, subject, treat, effort_t1, effort_t2, demographics, and derived fields. Required input for the Python script.
files.data cleaning and analysis.do: Original Stata .do file included for provenance and to show original cleaning logic and variable names. Not executed directly in this replication; instead logic was reproduced in the Python script. Location: data/original/28/input/replication_data/data cleaning and analysis.do.
files.DataDictionary.csv: Variable definitions mapping columns to meanings (useful for confirming interpretation of 'treat' and 'effort_t1'). Location: data/original/28/input/replication_data/DataDictionary.csv.
files.raw_data/: Folder containing multiple raw z-Tree-exported CSV files per session. Not required to run the replication since FinalData.csv is the cleaned/merged dataset; included for provenance.

codebase.notes: I translated the Stata .do processing (which referenced desktop paths) into a Python script that uses absolute /app/data paths. The Python script reads input from /app/data/original/28/input/replication_data/FinalData.csv and writes outputs to /app/data/replication_results.json and /app/data/replication_results.csv. Ensure that the container or execution environment mounts the host replication data under /app/data. Dependencies: pandas, numpy, scipy, statsmodels (if OLS regression is executed). The original .do file remains unchanged and is provided for documentation of the original cleaning logic.

analysis.instructions: 1) Ensure the host folder with replication data is mounted at /app/data (e.g., docker run -v $(pwd)/data:/app/data ... ). 2) Install Python dependencies (pip install pandas numpy scipy statsmodels). 3) Execute the script: python /app/data/original/28/input/replication_data/data_cleaning_and_analysis__py.py. 4) Review outputs: /app/data/replication_results.json contains numerical results and metadata; /app/data/replication_results.csv contains the summary by treatment. 5) Compare the computed OS and TS first-stage means, medians, Mann-Whitney U statistic and p-value to the original reported values (initial_details.txt and post_registration.json report OS mean ~71.7, TS first-stage mean ~84.8, Mann-Whitney two-sided p = 0.011). Document any differences and possible reasons (sample size, missing sessions, variable coding).
analysis.comparison_metrics: Primary comparison metrics: mean(effort_t1) by treatment, median(effort_t1) by treatment, mean difference (TS - OS), Mann-Whitney U statistic and two-sided p-value, Welch t-test statistic and p-value, Cohen's d, and OLS treatment coefficient with robust standard errors (if regression performed). Compare these to original reported numbers: OS mean 71.7, TS first-stage mean 84.8, TS vs OS difference 13.1 points with p=0.011 (post_registration.json, initial_details.txt).
